name: FF Meta
classification_notes: Humanist sans-serif conceived by Spiekermann as a deliberate antithesis to Helvetica. Originally designed for Deutsche Bundespost (West German postal service) in 1985 but rejected; released commercially via FontFont in 1991. Angled crossbar on M, asymmetrical E and T, open apertures. Expanded to include FF Meta Serif (2007), FF Meta Headline (2005) and FF Meta Variable (2018).
summary: Erik Spiekermann's humanist sans-serif — born from a rejected postal commission and released through FontFont in 1991 to become one of the most influential typefaces of the 1990s.
description: FF Meta was designed in 1985 for Deutsche Bundespost but shelved before release. Spiekermann revived it for FontFont in 1991, intending it as an alternative to the then-ubiquitous Helvetica — warmer, more distinctive and more legible at small sizes. Its angled M crossbar, asymmetrical horizontals and open apertures became defining features of 1990s graphic design. The family expanded to include serif, headline and variable versions.
foundry_blurb: FF Meta is Erik Spiekermann's most celebrated typeface — a humanist sans designed as a functional antithesis of Helvetica, with warmth and legibility built into its letterforms from the start.

name: FF DIN
classification_notes: Industrial grotesque drawn from the German standard DIN 1451. Oblique rather than true italic. Square dot above lowercase i. Rounded shoulder on lowercase r. Straight leg on uppercase R. Expanded over time with Cyrillic, Greek and Eastern European sets. FF DIN Round (2010) offers five weights with rounded terminals.
summary: Albert-Jan Pool's FontFont interpretation of the German DIN standard letterform — industrial, precise and widely used in transportation and corporate identity.
description: FF DIN was designed by Albert-Jan Pool in 1995, drawing on the official German DIN 1451 standard used for road and technical signage. Pool refined the industrial source material into a typographically coherent family with consistent spacing and extended character sets. Its precise, unornamented character has made it a staple of transport, technology and corporate design. FF DIN Round, added in 2010, provides a friendlier alternative.
foundry_blurb: FF DIN transforms the utilitarian DIN letterform standard into a fully realised type family — disciplined, technical and deeply functional across print and screen.

name: FF Scala
classification_notes: Old-style serif originally commissioned for Muziekcentrum Vredenburg in Utrecht. Named after Teatro alla Scala, Milan. Features small caps, ligatures, text figures and condensed variants. FF Scala Sans (1993) created a matching sans companion, forming one of the early superfamily pairings. Reissued under Majoor's own foundry in 2023.
summary: Martin Majoor's old-style serif, originally designed for a Dutch concert hall and notable as one of the first serif-and-sans superfamilies.
description: FF Scala was released through FontFont in 1991, designed by Martin Majoor for Muziekcentrum Vredenburg in Utrecht. Drawing on historic models including Dwiggins's Electra and Gill's Joanna, it has a warmly classical character. The addition of FF Scala Sans in 1993 made it one of the first serif-plus-sans superfamilies. It has been used by KLM and the US Department of Homeland Security, and was reissued under Majoor's own foundry in 2023.
foundry_blurb: FF Scala is a cultured old-style serif designed for cultural institutions — disciplined and legible in text, refined in display, and paired from the beginning with its own sans companion.

name: FF Dax
classification_notes: Humanist sans-serif with spurless terminal design — strokes end without decorative flourishes. Published by FontFont between 1995 and 1997. Refined in 2005 with FF Daxline. Used by UPS (as UPS Sans), New Scientist and political organisations.
summary: Hans Reichel's spurless humanist sans-serif, widely used in corporate identity and editorial work through the late 1990s and 2000s.
description: FF Dax was designed by Hans Reichel and released through FontFont between 1995 and 1997. Its spurless terminal construction — strokes that end without any decorative flare — gives it a modernist abstraction while the humanist proportions keep it readable. UPS adopted a modified version as UPS Sans in 2003. Reichel refined the design in 2005, producing FF Daxline as a cleaner text-oriented version.
foundry_blurb: FF Dax is a confident, contemporary sans with spurless terminals and humanist proportions — clean enough for corporate work, characterful enough for editorial applications.

name: FF Quadraat
classification_notes: Old-style serif designed by Fred Smeijers with a strong typographic rigour. Features small caps and old-style figures. FF Quadraat Sans released as a companion. Notable for its ink-trap inspired details and robust text colour.
summary: Fred Smeijers's rigorous old-style serif, released through FontFont in 1992 with robust text colour and ink-trap-influenced details.
description: FF Quadraat was designed by Fred Smeijers and released through FontFont in 1992. Rooted in old-style serif tradition with a Dutch approach to proportion and detail, it features pronounced ink-trap influenced details and strong text colour. FF Quadraat Sans followed as a companion, making it a full superfamily. Smeijers is also known for his type-historical writing, and that scholarship shows in Quadraat's rigour.
foundry_blurb: FF Quadraat is a seriously considered old-style — Fred Smeijers at his most typographically disciplined, with ink-trap details and a robustness that serves long-form text.

name: FF Beowolf
designer: Erik van Blokland, Just van Rossum
classification_notes: One of the first fonts released through FontFont and among the earliest 'randomised' typefaces. Used PostScript programming to introduce controlled randomness into letterforms — each character printed slightly differently. A landmark of digital type experimentation.
summary: Erik van Blokland and Just van Rossum's PostScript-randomised typeface — one of FontFont's founding releases and a landmark of experimental digital type.
description: FF Beowolf was released at FontFont's founding in 1990 and is one of the most conceptually significant typefaces of the digital era. Van Blokland and van Rossum used PostScript programming to introduce controlled randomness into every character — each letter was slightly different from the last, mimicking hand-drawn or letterpress variability. It established LettError's reputation and demonstrated that type could be a system rather than a fixed set of forms.
foundry_blurb: FF Beowolf is a living typeface — built on PostScript code that randomises its letterforms on every output, making each printed character unique within a defined range.

name: FF Kievit
designer: Mike Abbink, Christian Schwartz, Paul van der Laan
classification_notes: Humanist sans-serif drawing from Frutiger and Univers but with its own refined character. Large x-height, open apertures optimised for small text sizes. 18 styles across 9 weights with true italics, small caps and text figures. Used by Autodesk and the University of California.
summary: A considered humanist sans from Abbink, Schwartz and van der Laan — 18 styles with a large x-height designed for demanding text environments.
description: FF Kievit was completed in 2001 after development began in 1995, designed by Mike Abbink, Christian Schwartz and Paul van der Laan. It draws on the legibility principles of Frutiger and Univers while adding humanist warmth from classic serifed traditions. True italics, small caps and text figures across nine weights make it well-equipped for complex publication design. It is used by Autodesk and the University of California.
foundry_blurb: FF Kievit is a thoroughly designed humanist sans-serif — built for demanding text applications with a comprehensive weight range, true italics and small caps across the family.
